marcus_scaurus_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: Close the door. Close it. Sit down. I — forgive me. My hands won't stop. They killed Drusus. A leather-worker's knife in his own vestibule — his own vestibule, where his children play. No trial. No proscription. No senatus consultum. Just a knife in the dark and the best man in this body bleeding out on his floor tiles while his slaves screamed for a physician who was never going to arrive in time. I told him, Crispus. I told him the Gracchi died, Saturninus died, the pattern holds. He looked at me with that damnable smile and said the pattern was made by men and men could break it. He was wrong. And now I must say something I have never said aloud in this building — I who have spoken from the rostra in flood and fire and famine. I am frightened.

responses.playerDialogue: Then the law has already failed. If the Republic cannot protect a tribune in his own home, what exactly have we been defending?
I grieve with you. He deserved better. Rome deserved better than to learn it could do this to its own.
Who held the knife? Give me a name, Princeps, and I will make the pattern run the other direction.
You're frightened because you can see what follows. Tell me what you see — all of it.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I don't — I have no words for this. Everything is different now.

marcus_scaurus_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: What I see. Yes. You recall what I told you — the system built on custom, on consensus, on the unspoken agreement that there are things Roman men do not do to each other? That system has no enforcement mechanism. I have known this for decades. I chose to believe — perhaps the word is 'needed to believe' — that the consensus itself was wall enough. That men would choose to be bound because they understood the alternative. Drusus's murder has shown me what I already knew and could not face: the consensus is dead. Custom restrains only men who feel restrained by it, and we have raised a generation that feels restrained by nothing. The Italians will revolt — they have no earthly reason not to. And when they do, the Senate will hand extraordinary commands to its generals and pray those commands come back. They won't. That is what I see.

options.playerDialogue: And when the commands don't come back — what then?

marcus_scaurus_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: Names. Everyone wants names. As if this were a crime with a culprit — as if one man's arrest could stitch the wound closed. But I will tell you something worse than the name of the man who held the knife: the Senate is relieved. Oh, not publicly — publicly they are wringing their hands and calling for investigations that will investigate nothing. But in the cloakrooms, at the private dinners, I can read their faces. One less reformer. One less disruption to the comfortable order. They don't understand — they cannot see — that the comfortable order died on those floor tiles with him. The allies watched their champion murdered. They will draw the obvious conclusion.

responses.playerDialogue: Then the Senate is complicit. Not in the blade — in the silence afterward. That's the deeper crime.
Is there still time? Could something be offered to the allies — enough to pull them back from what you're describing?
Then we prepare for war. Not because we choose it — because it chose us the moment that knife fell.
The allies aren't the only ones drawing conclusions. Every ambitious man in Rome just learned that working within the system is a death sentence.

marcus_scaurus_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: What comes next is the thing I have spent my entire career trying to prevent. A man with legions discovers — not suspects, not theorizes, discovers — that the Senate cannot stop him. Not that it chooses not to. That it cannot. The customs were the only wall and the wall is down. Marius already knows this. He has known it since Numidia. Sulla is learning it. And when two such men both understand that nothing stands between them and what they want except each other — that is not a republic. That is a battlefield with marble columns. I am telling you this, Crispus, because someone must carry this understanding past my death. I will not live to see what comes after — my physician's face tells me that much even when his words do not. Will you remember what I have taught you?

options.playerDialogue: I will remember. And I will do everything I can to rebuild what's being lost — even inside a system that no longer believes in itself.
I will remember. But I won't pretend the old walls can be raised again. Something new will have to be built from whatever stone survives.

marcus_scaurus_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: Then you are what I hoped you were. Take this — my seal. I have given it twice before in my life. When you press it into wax before any man of the optimate faction, he will know you carry the authority of the Princeps Senatus. I do not give it lightly. I do not give it happily — I give it because there is no time left for caution. Use it to preserve what can still be preserved. And when they write the history of these years — make certain they write that Marcus Aemilius Scaurus saw the thing coming and spent his last strength trying to stop it. That is all any of us can do. Go now. I have letters to write that I hoped — I prayed to every god in this city — I would never have to write.

marcus_scaurus_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Something new. Yes. Perhaps you are right, and the old man's faith in the old ways is itself a species of blindness — the last and most dignified kind. I cannot follow you into whatever comes next. My mind was made for the Republic that is dying, not for what replaces it. But I am glad I told you these things. Take the knowledge. Use it to build something I would not recognize, if you must. The understanding of why a thing breaks is always worth preserving — even when the thing itself is not.

operations.reason: Scaurus's personal seal grants you authority within the optimate network

deltas.reason: Accepted the burden of constitutional memory from the Princeps Senatus

exitStates.narrativeLabel: Witness to the Old Man's Warning

deltas.reason: Absorbed Scaurus's constitutional analysis without binding yourself to his conclusions
